question_title: Will the CDC report at least 2,500 confirmed measles cases in the United States for 2026 before May 1, 2026?
summary: The CDC reports 1,671 confirmed measles cases as of April 2, 2026. Reaching the 2,500 threshold before May 1 requires 829 additional cases in approximately 4 weekly CDC updates (~207/week), which is roughly double the recent average rate of ~100/week. The largest outbreak driver (South Carolina, ~1,196 outbreak-associated cases) has had zero new cases for 2 weeks. Arizona is also slowing. Utah is the primary current growth engine at ~73 cases/week, but even with other scattered outbreaks, the total weekly rate has been flat around 80-100. Monte Carlo simulations consistently show very low probability (0-6.4%) of reaching 2,500. The main tail risk is an explosive new outbreak in a large unvaccinated community, but such outbreaks need 2-3 weeks to ramp up, leaving insufficient time. I estimate ~7% probability.

factors.description: Current count (1,671) requires 829 more cases in ~4 weeks at ~207/week, roughly 2x the recent average rate of ~100/week
South Carolina outbreak (largest driver, ~1,196 outbreak-associated cases) has had NO new cases for 2 weeks, removing the primary growth engine
Recent weekly trend is flat/slightly declining (145, 81, 125, 88, 96) with no sign of the acceleration needed to reach ~207/week
Arizona outbreak also slowing significantly (only 1 new case last week)
Utah outbreak actively growing (~73/week, 142 in past 3 weeks), demonstrating measles can still spread rapidly in unvaccinated communities
92% of cases in unvaccinated individuals - remaining pockets of unvaccinated populations across the US create potential for new outbreaks
17 active outbreaks across 33 states - widespread distribution provides multiple potential acceleration points
Measles has very high R0 (12-18) enabling explosive growth, but new outbreaks need 2-3 weeks to ramp up - tight timeline for meaningful impact before May 1

reasoning:
**Forecast: 7% probability (NO is strongly favored)**

Key reasoning:
- **Current state**: 1,671 confirmed cases as of April 2, needing 829 more to reach 2,500
- **Rate gap**: Requires ~207 cases/week for 4 weeks — roughly **2x the recent ~100/week rate**
- **Outbreaks winding down**: South Carolina (the largest driver) has reported **zero new cases for 2 weeks**; Arizona is also slowing
- **Utah is growing** but at ~73/week, far from enough to close the gap alone
- **No new major outbreaks** emerging that could drive the needed acceleration
- **Monte Carlo models** consistently show 0-6.4% probability; scenario analysis gives ~9.7%

The 7% reflects genuine tail risk from measles' explosive potential in unvaccinated communities, while respecting that the data shows deceleration, not the acceleration needed.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the CDC report at least 2,500 confirmed measles cases in the United States for 2026 before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43058)
[2026 US measles total nears 1,000 as South Carolina confirms 11 new cases](https://www.cidrap.umn.edu/measles/2026-us-measles-total-nears-1000-south-carolina-confirms-11-new-cases)

[US measles cases soar to 588 so far this year as South Carolina confirms 58 new infections](https://www.cidrap.umn.edu/measles/us-measles-cases-soar-588-so-far-year-south-carolina-confirms-58-new-infections)

[Measles outbreaks slow in South Carolina, Arizona, but Utah sees cases skyrocket past 400](https://www.cidrap.umn.edu/measles/measles-outbreaks-slow-south-carolina-arizona-utah-sees-cases-skyrocket-past-400)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the CDC measles page at https://www.cdc.gov/measles/data-research/index.html shows a cumulative 2026 confirmed case count of 2500 or more before May 1, 2026. Otherwise, this question resolves to no.
fine_print: Use the CDC's cumulative confirmed U.S. case count for 2026, not suspected cases and not a state subtotal. Revisions posted before May 1, 2026 count. Candidate Sources: none
